one_line_takeaway: FIRE mitigates backdoors at inference time by identifying an attack-specific linear 'trigger direction' in a network's latent space and subtracting it from poisoned representations, recovering near-clean accuracy for most of 21 tested attack/dataset/architecture configurations while running roughly 100x faster per sample than the diffusion-based ZIP baseline.
key_finding: The layer-wise study (Table 1, 21 dataset-attack-architecture configurations) shows the optimal repair depth is attack-dependent and consistent across datasets/architectures: e.g. WaNet is best mitigated at latent space 2 (poor at 0/1); BadNets and Blended are best mitigated at early latent spaces; InputAware needs the deepest latent space (4) and still leaves a 13.41-percentage-point gap on GTSRB/PreActResNet18 (PA 84.10% vs CA 97.51%, the worst case reported). For over half the 21 configurations, FIRE recovers the true label within 1 percentage point of Clean Accuracy at the best layer (e.g. BadNets on CIFAR-10/PreActResNet18: CA 91.84%, undefended PA 5.27%, corrected PA 91.12-91.66% across layers, diff-to-CA of 0.18%). The authors note this contradicts Jebreel et al. (2024), who found backdoor signal most separable in the second half of the network; FIRE instead finds many attacks (Blended, BadNets) are best purified in early layers. In the streaming benchmark (Table 2, same 21 configs), FIRE outperforms ZIP in 9/21 settings at the very first poisoned sample and in all 21/21 settings by the 10th poisoned sample, because FIRE's online direction estimate improves with more samples while ZIP's diffusion-based correction is static per-sample. On timing, FIRE's one-time online initialization takes roughly ~200-250 ms, after which each subsequent sample is processed in ~11-27 ms, versus ZIP's ~1.4-1.6 s per sample -- roughly a two-order-of-magnitude per-sample speedup.
core_contribution: The paper introduces FIRE (Feature-space Inference-time REpair), an inference-time backdoor mitigation method for deployed (already-trained, weight-frozen) deep neural networks. The authors hypothesize and empirically show that a backdoor trigger induces a structured, approximately linear displacement in the model's latent representation at some layer depth, and that this displacement is consistent across poisoned samples. FIRE estimates this 'general trigger direction' at one or more candidate latent spaces (via paired clean/poisoned samples, unpaired centroid differences, or an augmentation-based proxy that exploits the fragility of trigger features under corruptions such as color jitter and Gaussian blur) and repairs a poisoned input at inference time by subtracting the estimated direction from its latent representation before continuing the forward pass, without retraining, fine-tuning, or modifying model weights. For the deployment setting, FIRE is instantiated as a streaming/online algorithm (Alg. 1) that maintains running estimates of the trigger direction per candidate latent space and applies repair at the earliest layer whose correction flips the model's prediction, improving its correction quality as more flagged poisoned samples arrive.
threat_model: Attacker: has full control over the training pipeline/data (standard backdoor setting) and implants a hidden trigger so the model behaves normally on clean inputs but predicts an attacker-chosen target label when the trigger is present. Defender: operates purely at inference time on an already-deployed, potentially backdoored model; has access to (1) a small set of clean reference samples (Nc=100 by default) and (2) inference/white-box-style access to the deployed model sufficient to compute predictions and extract intermediate-layer activations for selected layers during a forward pass, but cannot access original training data, cannot modify model weights, and cannot afford retraining, fine-tuning, or expensive optimization-based purification. The runtime setting assumes a stream of inputs already flagged as poisoned by an upstream black-box detector (e.g., SCALE-UP), and abstention on flagged inputs is not acceptable (motivated by an autonomous-driving traffic-sign example), so the defense must always output a corrected label rather than reject the input.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: evaluation is currently restricted to image classification models (CIFAR-10, GTSRB with PreActResNet18/34); extending FIRE to other domains (text, graphs) and to multi-objective/multi-trigger backdoors that may require repair at multiple latent spaces simultaneously is left to future work. The method also assumes an upstream detector has already flagged inputs as poisoned (studied further, per the paper, in an appendix on imperfect detectors not reviewed here) and assumes availability of a small clean reference set, which is a nontrivial defender-capability assumption. The worst-case correction gap (InputAware on GTSRB, 13.41 points below Clean Accuracy) shows the method is not uniformly near-perfect across all attack types.

quantitative_results: Table 1: BadNets/CIFAR-10/PreActResNet18 -- CA 91.84%, undefended PA 5.27%, best-layer FIRE PA up to 91.66%, diff to CA 0.18%
Table 1: InputAware/GTSRB/PreActResNet18 -- CA 97.51%, undefended PA 6.17%, best-layer FIRE PA 84.10%, diff to CA 13.41% (worst case in the study)
Table 1: for over half of the 21 tested configurations, FIRE recovers PA within 1 percentage point of CA at the best latent space
Table 2 (streaming): FIRE outperforms ZIP in 9/21 configurations at the 1st poisoned sample and in 21/21 configurations by the 10th poisoned sample
Table 2: FIRE online per-sample processing time ~11-27 ms after an initialization of ~200-250 ms, vs ZIP ~1.4-1.6 s per sample (measured on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU)
5 candidate latent spaces used per architecture (after the first convolutional layer and after each residual block)
Poisoning ratio of 10% used to train backdoored models across 7 attack methods
baselines_compared: ZIP (Shi et al., 2023) -- diffusion-based zero-shot black-box test-time image purification, the sole identified prior inference-time method that corrects rather than rejects predictions without training additional components
